questions.question: Gold sells for $1,950 per ounce in Market A and $1,980 per ounce in Market B. Transaction costs are $10 per ounce total. What arbitrage action and profit per ounce are available?

choices.A: Buy in A and sell in B; profit is $20 per ounce.
choices.B: Buy in B and sell in A; profit is $20 per ounce.
choices.C: Buy in A and sell in B; profit is $30 per ounce.
choices.D: No arbitrage exists because prices differ by less than $50.

questions.explanation: Buy at 1,950, sell at 1,980, and pay 10 in transaction costs, giving 20 profit. B reverses the trade, C ignores transaction costs, and D imposes an arbitrary threshold.

questions.question: A risk-free claim pays $1,100 in one year. The interest rate is 10%, but the claim sells for $970. What arbitrage strategy earns riskless profit today?

choices.A: Sell the claim short and invest $970 at 10%.
choices.B: Borrow $970, buy the claim, and repay $1,067 next year, keeping $33 next year.
choices.C: Buy the claim with cash only; no borrowing is involved, so profit is not arbitrage.
choices.D: Borrow $1,100, buy the claim, and repay $970 next year.

questions.explanation: The no-arbitrage price is 1,100/1.1 = 1,000. Borrowing 970 to buy the underpriced claim requires repayment of 1,067, leaving 33 next year risk-free. A shorts an underpriced asset, C misses the zero-net-investment arbitrage structure, and D reverses loan repayment logic.

questions.question: If many traders exploit an arbitrage opportunity by buying an underpriced asset, what happens to the asset's price and expected excess return?

choices.A: Price rises and excess return falls toward zero.
choices.B: Price falls and excess return rises.
choices.C: Price rises and excess return rises because demand is higher.
choices.D: Price stays fixed because arbitrage affects only quantities.

questions.explanation: Buying pressure raises price; with fixed payoff, the return falls until no excess return remains. B and C get the price-return adjustment wrong, and D ignores price adjustment in competitive markets.

questions.question: Two identical one-year risk-free claims pay $500. Claim X costs $450 and Claim Y costs $470. What happens as arbitrageurs trade?

choices.A: They buy X and sell Y, pushing X's price up and Y's price down.
choices.B: They buy Y and sell X, pushing Y's price up and X's price down.
choices.C: They buy both because both have positive payoffs.
choices.D: They sell both because identical payoffs cannot have positive value.

questions.explanation: The cheaper identical claim is underpriced relative to the expensive one. Arbitrageurs buy X and sell Y, narrowing the price gap. B reverses the trade, C ignores relative mispricing, and D denies asset value.

questions.question: A graph plots the price difference between two identical assets over time. The difference starts at $12 and moves toward $0 as trading volume rises. What does this graph illustrate?

choices.A: Arbitrage widens price gaps because traders prefer cheap assets.
choices.B: Arbitrage pressure eliminates price differences for identical payoffs.
choices.C: Arbitrage requires prices to remain permanently different.
choices.D: Arbitrage works only when trading volume falls.

questions.explanation: Trading on the price difference pushes prices together until the riskless profit disappears. A reverses the effect, C contradicts no-arbitrage, and D misreads the role of trading volume.

questions.question: In a payoff diagram, Portfolio A and Portfolio B have identical future payoffs in every possible state, but Portfolio A is plotted with a lower current cost. What does the graph imply?

choices.A: Buy A and sell B; the vertical cost gap is riskless arbitrage profit.
choices.B: Buy B and sell A; higher current cost means higher value.
choices.C: Hold both portfolios because identical future payoffs require diversification.
choices.D: No conclusion is possible because payoffs are identical.

questions.explanation: Identical future payoffs must have the same current price. If A is cheaper, buying A and selling B gives the same future obligations/assets with money left over today. B reverses the trade, C is irrelevant, and D misses the no-arbitrage implication.

questions.question: Why cannot persistent arbitrage opportunities survive in competitive markets?

choices.A: Because traders exploiting riskless profit change demand and supply, pushing prices to no-arbitrage levels.
choices.B: Because governments set all asset prices equal.
choices.D: Because arbitrage opportunities are profitable only after prices adjust.

questions.explanation: Competitive arbitrage creates buying and selling pressure that removes mispricing. B is not the general market mechanism, C is false, and D reverses the timing.

questions.question: A concert ticket can be bought on one platform for $80 and immediately resold on another for $95. Platform fees total $8 per ticket. Which framework applies, and what is the profit?

choices.B: Arbitrage; riskless profit is $7 per ticket before considering any resale risk.
choices.D: Monopoly pricing; profit is $95 - marginal cost.

questions.explanation: The same good can be bought low and sold high immediately, giving 95 - 80 - 8 = 7. A confuses profit with utility, C inserts an irrelevant interest rate, and D uses a seller-pricing model.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes arbitrage from ordinary risky investment?

choices.A: Arbitrage requires a riskless profit from price differences or mispricing; risky investment earns compensation for bearing uncertainty.
choices.B: Arbitrage always loses money, while risky investment always earns money.
choices.C: Arbitrage means buying any asset expected to rise in price.
choices.D: Arbitrage requires patience, while risky investment requires no time.

questions.explanation: Arbitrage is riskless profit from inconsistent prices; risky investment involves uncertain payoffs. B is false, C is too broad, and D confuses time horizon with risk.

questions.question: A portfolio that pays $100 in every state next year costs $92. A risk-free bond paying $100 next year costs $95. What arbitrage profit is available today?

choices.B: $3 by buying the portfolio and selling the bond short.
choices.C: $3 by buying the bond and selling the portfolio short.

questions.explanation: The two assets have identical future payoffs, so buy the cheaper portfolio for 92 and short the bond for 95, receiving 3 today with no future net obligation. A ignores current price differences, C reverses the arbitrage, and D measures payoff minus cost rather than arbitrage against an identical asset.
